post: 250 years ago this summer, a resolution was read aloud to the Continental Congress that changed the world. The generation in classrooms right now will inherit that story. Are they ready to own it? At the Bill of Rights Institute, we're not waiting until July 4th to answer that question. We've been building toward this moment, and the resources are almost here. Here's a preview of what's coming for teachers and students before the ink dries on America's 250th birthday: - A physical + digital A250 curriculum kit - "Decoding the Declaration" – a document-based activity that puts students inside the founding moment - A full mini-documentary playlist already live on YouTube (check it out:  https://bit.ly/4s3ssll) - A dedicated A250 newsletter and resource hub with an American Revolution timeline This is the school year civic educators have been waiting for. Read what we have lined up, and make sure your students don't miss it. 🔗  https://bit.ly/4b95G5O

This is the school year civics teachers have been waiting for. America's 250th anniversary is a teaching moment that won't come around again in our lifetimes. The Civic Star Challenge exists to capture what's happening in classrooms right now, during this historic year. Share your Declaration classroom moment. Enter the weekly $300 drawing. And help build a record of what civic education looks like in 2026.  https://bit.ly/463KXxf  #CivicStarChallenge  #America250  #CivicEducation  #HistoryTeachers  #Semiquincentennial

We budget for subscriptions we barely use. What about 1 tool students WILL use? Civics Champions fund Pocket Constitutions for classrooms, starting at $1 per copy. Share with someone who cares about civic learning:  https://bit.ly/4rzKQC5  #CivicsChampion  #CivicEducation  #Constitution  #Giving

1 man. 1 laundry. 1 case that changed American constitutional law forever. In 1886, a Chinese immigrant named Lee Yick fought San Francisco's discriminatory enforcement of a laundry ordinance all the way to the U. S. Supreme Court… and won. His story is a landmark lesson in the 14th Amendment, equal protection, and the courage it takes to hold the line when the system is stacked against you. BRI Vice President Kirk Higgins tells the story on Our American Stories with Lee Habeeb. 🎧  https://ihr.fm/3NmEmHT  #CivicsEducation  #EqualProtection  #AmericanHistory  #BillOfRightsInstitute

The 14th Amendment was "1 step forward, 2 steps back" for women's suffrage. Section One declared all persons born on U. S. soil to be citizens, a victory women's suffragists quickly seized on. If women were citizens, didn't voting follow? But Section Two inserted the word "male" into the Constitution for the first time, explicitly in the context of voting rights. Then came the proposed 15th Amendment, prohibiting voting discrimination based on race… but not sex. It forced a fracture. 3 distinct positions emerged among reformers who had once stood together. Frederick Douglass and Lucy Stone argued this was the Black man's hour, a matter of life and death that had to come first, even if universal suffrage wasn't yet politically possible. Anthony and Stanton refused, warning it would be another 70 years before women gained the vote, and argued, in terms history rightly judges harshly, that White women should not be passed over for Black men. And Black women like Sojourner Truth and Frances Harper asked a question neither camp was answering: why was no one talking about Black women? Before Reconstruction, abolitionists and women's suffragists had marched together. The 14th and 15th Amendments shattered that unity for decades, until a new generation stepped forward to rebuild it. Telling the full story of America means telling this part too. Episode 5 of The Women's Declaration with Savannah Eccles Johnston, PhD (@savypolitics on IG) is live now 🎬 ▶️  https://bit.ly/4rqWkXC  #WomensHistoryMonth  #15thAmendment  #WomensSuffrage  #SojournerTruth  #FrederickDouglass  #AmericanHistory  #CivicsEducation  #America250

Your students already have opinions about college athlete pay. The question is whether they can defend them. The debate over whether college athletes should be treated as employees has moved from ESPN panels to federal courts, and it's one of the most compelling real-world examples of constitutional reasoning your students will encounter all year. Labor rights. Due process. The relationship between individuals and institutions. The limits of what a contract – in this case, a scholarship – actually covers. These are on the front pages right now. Think the Vote's latest debate – Should College Athletes Be Treated as Employees of Their Universities? – gives students a structured space to examine both sides of this question with the evidence and civic reasoning skills they'll carry long after the final bell. If you believe the next generation of citizens deserves better than bumper-sticker arguments, this is the resource for them. 👉 Explore the debate:  https://bit.ly/4rthRPH  #CivicsEducation  #ThinktheVote  #SocialStudies  #HighSchoolTeachers  #CollegeAthletes  #NIL  #CriticalThinking  #BillofRightsInstitute

Most teachers aren't recognized for the moments that matter most, the ones that happen between the textbook and the test. The Civic Star Challenge is BRI's way of changing that. Share what's happening in your classroom around America's 250th Anniversary, and you're automatically entered in a weekly $300 prize drawing. You've already done the hard part. All we're asking is that you share it. This week's drawing is still open:  https://bit.ly/463KXxf  #CivicStarChallenge  #CivicsTeacher  #HistoryTeacher  #America250  #TeacherRecognition

He got a C on the paper. Then he changed the U. S. Constitution. In 1982, a University of Texas student named Gregory Watson discovered something most Americans had no idea about: one of James Madison's original proposed amendments had never been ratified, and technically, it still could be. His professor wasn't impressed. Watson was. What followed is one of the most remarkable stories of individual civic action in American history, and a reminder that the Constitution isn't a relic. It's a living document, still being shaped by citizens willing to act. Read the full story 👇 🔗  https://bit.ly/40paTjx

Move over, every famous duo in history. The greatest power couple in American political history was Elizabeth Cady Stanton and Susan B. Anthony. 70 years after the signing of the Declaration of Independence, Stanton shocked the nation at the Seneca Falls Convention in 1848, calling for women's suffrage and authoring the Declaration of Sentiments. The document was a deliberate reimagining of the Declaration of Independence: instead of targeting King George for trampling the rights of colonists, it targeted men for trampling the rights of women. It was a radical, fringe political statement. And it was going nowhere… Until Anthony. Where Stanton dropped philosophical bombs and went home to her 7 children, Anthony built the machinery. She was a relentless political organizer who took Stanton's ideas and turned them into a national movement, making women's suffrage seem "almost inevitable." Neither lived to see the 19th Amendment. But without their 5 decades of friendship and partnership, there is no women's suffrage movement in the United States. Episode 4 of The Women's Declaration with Savannah Eccles Johnston, PhD (@savypolitics on IG) is live now 🎬 ▶️  https://bit.ly/3NuU6bV  #WomensHistoryMonth  #SusanBAnthony  #ElizabethCadyStanton  #SenecaFalls  #WomensSuffrage  #America250  #CivicsEducation

One class raised funds to build a quiet corner in their school, a simple space for students who get overstimulated. Another student created an entire accessible tennis program for children with disabilities. He won the $10,000 grand prize. Both were recognized by the MyImpact Challenge. Both were valid. This is worth sitting with, because one of the most consistent barriers to entry, for students and for teachers, is the belief that a project has to be extraordinary to qualify. It doesn't. A lemonade stand fundraiser. A neighborhood cleanup. A book drive. An entrepreneurial club. A nonprofit. The MyImpact Challenge recognizes the full spectrum of student-led community impact, because the point isn't scale, it's that students take real, meaningful action. What's particularly worth knowing for educators: The MyImpact Challenge works exceptionally well as a whole-class project. Groups of up to 5 students can submit a single entry together. AP Government courses, service learning programs, and gifted/talented classes have all used it as an applied civics experience that gives students a real framework for the skills they're building in the classroom. There's $40,000 in total prizes, including awards for teachers whose students enter. Every student who submits a valid entry receives recognition. Students ages 13-19 in all 50 states, U. S. territories, and Armed Forces Schools Abroad can participate. If your students are doing the work, they deserve to be recognized for it. 👉  https://bit.ly/3fyGGr3  #MyImpactChallenge  #CivicsEducation  #ServiceLearning  #APGovernment  #BillOfRightsInstitute
